questions.q: A public developer portal exposes an API in Exchange. The API team wants every consuming application to be known, individually revocable, and approved by an API owner before receiving access. Which platform workflow provides this?
options: Consumers request access under a contract an owner approves and can revoke per app
Publish the API's single shared API key on the public portal page for every consumer
Add consumers' office IP ranges to an allowlist policy
Send credentials by email to developers who ask
optionNotes: Correct. The Exchange 'Request access' flow creates a per-application contract: unique client credentials per consumer, an approval step when tiers are set to manual, per-application analytics attribution, and one-click revocation — governance at consumer granularity.
One shared key means no attribution and revocation punishes everyone at once.
IP allowlists identify networks, not applications, and crumble with cloud/NAT consumers.
Manual credential distribution scales poorly and leaves no platform-managed contract to revoke.
questions.explanation: Contract-based access is the governed on-ramp: register an application, request access (optionally owner-approved), receive unique credentials enforced by client ID/SLA policies. Every consumer becomes individually visible, measurable, and revocable.

questions.q: An API's implementation is a legacy .NET service that cannot run Mule policies natively. The organization still wants API Manager policies (rate limiting, client ID enforcement) applied. What is the standard approach?
options: Front it with a Mule proxy or Flex Gateway and manage its policies in API Manager
Rewrite the .NET service as a Mule application first
Implement the policies inside the .NET code by hand
Skip governance for non-Mule implementations
optionNotes: Correct. Policies need an enforcement point. For non-Mule backends that point is a proxy: API Manager can generate a Mule proxy app (deployable to CloudHub/RTF) or, more lightweight, Flex Gateway fronts the service — either way the API instance's policies apply without touching the backend.
A rewrite is a massive detour when only governance is needed.
Hand-rolled policy code diverges from platform-managed policies and loses central control, analytics, and updatability.
Ungoverned APIs are the exact gap the architecture is meant to close.
questions.explanation: Basic endpoint (Mule app with autodiscovery enforcing its own policies) vs proxy endpoint (gateway in front of any HTTP backend): the proxy pattern extends API Manager governance to implementations that cannot embed the gateway themselves.

questions.q: A process API implementation needs substantial internal changes (refactoring plus a library upgrade) with no change to its RAML contract. What should consumers experience when the new implementation is deployed to the same API instance?
options: Nothing — same endpoint and contract; interface and implementation are decoupled
They must re-request access because contracts reset on redeploy
They must switch to a new URL containing the build number
A mandatory one-hour maintenance outage
optionNotes: Correct. The API instance in API Manager — its endpoint, policies, SLA tiers, and consumer contracts — persists across implementation deployments. A contract-preserving reimplementation is invisible to consumers, which is the point of managing the interface separately from the code.
Contracts attach to the API instance, not to a particular deployment of its implementation.
Build-specific URLs would leak implementation lifecycle into the interface — the opposite of the model.
Zero-downtime deployment options exist precisely so internal changes need no outage.
questions.explanation: Interface lifecycle ≠ implementation lifecycle: consumers bind to the managed API instance (contract + endpoint + policies). Implementations behind it can be refactored, re-platformed, or scaled freely so long as the contract holds.

questions.q: A read-heavy system API serves country and currency reference data that changes monthly. Response times must improve and backend load must drop, without touching the implementation. Which policy achieves this?
options: An HTTP caching policy on the instance, serving repeated GETs with a TTL
A rate limiting policy set very low so that fewer requests ever reach the backend system
A JSON threat protection policy
Client ID enforcement, since identified traffic is faster
optionNotes: Correct. Gateway-side HTTP caching stores responses and answers repeat requests without invoking the implementation: latency drops to cache speed, backend load falls proportionally to the hit rate, and a TTL tuned to monthly volatility keeps staleness acceptable — all pure configuration.
Starving consumers reduces load by refusing service — it degrades the API rather than improving it.
Threat protection inspects payloads for attacks; it adds latency rather than removing it.
Identification has no caching effect.
questions.explanation: For cache-friendly workloads (GET, slow-changing data), the HTTP caching policy is the highest-leverage no-code optimization: applied at the enforcement point, invisible to the implementation, tunable per instance.

questions.q: An experience API aggregates three process APIs. Its 95th-percentile latency SLA is 800 ms. The three downstream calls are independent of each other, each with a p95 around 400 ms. Which implementation guidance follows?
options: Call the three in parallel (Scatter-Gather) so latency approaches the slowest, not the sum
Call the three APIs sequentially and hope caching absorbs the difference
Lower the SLA to 1500 ms to fit sequential calls
Merge the three process APIs into one so only one call is needed
optionNotes: Correct. Independent calls executed sequentially cost the sum (~1200 ms p95 — over budget); in parallel they cost roughly the max (~400–500 ms — comfortably within it). Per-call timeouts below the overall budget keep one slow dependency from blowing the SLA.
Sequential is structurally over budget; caching is a gamble, not a design.
Changing the requirement to fit a weaker design inverts the architect's job.
Merging deployed APIs to save network hops sacrifices layer reuse for a problem parallelism already solves.
questions.explanation: Latency budgeting is arithmetic: sequential independent calls add, parallel calls take the max. Parallel fan-out plus budget-aligned timeouts is the standard pattern for aggregating APIs under a latency SLA.

questions.q: A production API on CloudHub must remain available through the failure of a single worker AND the failure of an entire availability zone. What is the minimal deployment characteristic that provides this?
options: Deploy at least two workers — CloudHub spreads them across availability zones
One very large worker with auto-restart enabled
Two identical applications with DNS manually switched over on any failure event
A nightly backup of the application archive
optionNotes: Correct. Multi-worker CloudHub deployments are distributed across AZs within the region automatically; the shared (or dedicated) load balancer routes around a failed worker, and platform monitoring replaces it. Two workers is therefore the availability floor for production.
Auto-restart heals a crashed worker eventually, but requests fail during the gap, and an AZ outage takes the lone worker with it.
Manual DNS failover means minutes-to-hours of outage and human dependency.
Backups restore artifacts; they provide no runtime availability.
questions.explanation: On CloudHub, high availability is a worker-count decision: ≥2 workers gets automatic AZ distribution and load-balancer failover. Single-worker production deployments are the classic availability anti-pattern, whatever the worker's size.

questions.q: APIs deployed to an Anypoint VPC must be callable ONLY from the corporate network via the dedicated load balancer — direct calls to the workers' public cloudhub.io endpoints must be impossible. How is this enforced?
options: Listen only on the VPC-internal ports the DLB reaches, disabling the public route
Publish the APIs without any documentation so that outsiders simply cannot find them
Rely on client ID enforcement alone to keep outsiders out
Lower the workers' DNS TTL
optionNotes: Correct. In a VPC, apps choosing the internal listener ports (8091 http / 8092 https) are reachable only inside the VPC — i.e., through the DLB (and anything bridged via VPN/peering). Traffic through the public shared LB (which forwards to 8081/8082) has nothing listening, closing the side door.
Obscurity is not access control; cloudhub.io endpoints follow a guessable pattern.
Policies police identified API traffic but the public network path itself would remain open to probing.
DNS TTLs affect caching of lookups, not reachability.
questions.explanation: VPC port discipline is the standard lockdown: internal ports make the DLB the sole ingress, aligning network reality with the 'private API' intent. Firewall rules in the VPC can enforce the same at the network layer.

questions.q: A CloudHub 1.0 application handling live traffic must be updated without dropping in-flight or incoming requests. Which platform capability supports this?
options: Zero-downtime redeployment — cut over to new workers only once they are healthy
Stopping the application, deploying, and starting it quickly
Deploying at 3 AM when traffic is lower
Asking consumers to retry any failed requests during the deployment window themselves
optionNotes: Correct. CloudHub's zero-downtime update keeps the old workers serving while new-version workers boot and pass health checks; the load balancer then shifts traffic and old workers drain — no request-serving gap.
Stop-deploy-start guarantees an outage window, however brief.
Low traffic is still traffic; 'fewer failures' is not 'zero downtime'.
Pushing failure handling onto consumers concedes the requirement rather than meeting it.
questions.explanation: Zero-downtime deployment is a platform behaviour: parallel new-version workers, health-gated cutover, connection draining. It is the operational counterpart to interface/implementation decoupling — consumers never notice releases.

questions.q: An experience API calls a process API whose own SLA allows responses up to 30 seconds in the worst case, but the experience API must answer its clients within 3 seconds or return a graceful error. Which resilience configuration is correct?
options: Set the outbound timeout under the 3 s budget; map timeouts to a graceful error
Set the outbound response timeout to 30 seconds to match the downstream SLA exactly
Remove timeouts so no request is ever cut short
Retry the downstream call five times on timeout
optionNotes: Correct. Each layer's outbound timeout must fit inside its own promise to callers: roughly 2.5–2.8 s here, leaving margin to build the graceful degradation response. Waiting on a downstream that may lawfully take 30 s guarantees breaking the 3 s SLA.
Matching the downstream's worst case means inheriting it — 30 s of held connections and a blown SLA.
No timeout converts slow dependencies into resource exhaustion (held threads/connections) and indefinite client hangs.
Five sequential retries against a timing-out call multiplies the delay far past any budget.
questions.explanation: Timeout budgets cascade top-down: every outbound timeout must be smaller than the SLA the caller itself has promised, with headroom for fallback handling. Aligning to the dependency's SLA instead of your own is the classic latency-propagation mistake.

questions.q: An API owner wants to formalize with consumers not just endpoints and schemas, but expected availability, latency percentiles, and support hours. What is being defined, and where does the platform surface consumer-specific enforcement of usage limits?
options: The API's SLA (its NFRs); enforced per consumer via SLA tiers and policies
The RAML alone covers all of this; nothing else is needed
A firewall rule set, since all enforcement should happen at the network layer
A Slack channel agreement between the teams
optionNotes: Correct. Functional contract (RAML/OAS) and non-functional contract (SLA: availability, latency, support) are complementary halves of an API product. The measurable usage side — how much each consumer may call — is operationalized as SLA tiers with SLA-based policies enforcing them per application.
RAML expresses the functional interface; availability targets and support commitments live outside the schema language.
Firewalls know packets, not percentiles or business commitments.
Informal agreements are neither discoverable, measurable, nor enforceable.
questions.explanation: Treating an API as a product means publishing both contracts: the functional spec and the SLA. Anypoint Platform makes the consumption side concrete — tiers requested via Exchange, enforced by SLA policies, monitored by analytics.

questions.q: Product management asks: 'Which consumer applications drive the most traffic to our Orders API, which resources do they hit, and how did that change after last month's release?' Where does this information come from?
options: API analytics — per-client request metrics by resource and method over time
The load balancer's TCP connection counts
A survey sent to registered developer consumers
Worker CPU utilization graphs in Runtime Manager
optionNotes: Correct. Because governed APIs identify consumers (client ID enforcement/contracts), API analytics can slice request volume, latency, and errors by client application, API, resource, and time window — exactly the consumption insight requested.
TCP counts carry no API, resource, or consumer dimensions.
Surveys yield opinions, not measurements.
CPU graphs describe infrastructure load, not who called what.
questions.explanation: Consumer-attributable analytics are a payoff of governance: enforce client identification and every request becomes a data point tagged by application and resource — feeding adoption tracking, capacity planning, and product decisions about the application network.

questions.q: Operations must be paged when a production API instance's error rate or response time breaches thresholds — even if the underlying workers look healthy — and separately when a worker's CPU stays high. How should alerting be organized?
options: API-level alerts on the instance + infra alerts (CPU/memory) in Runtime Manager
A single CPU alert, since resource health implies API health
Grepping worker logs manually every morning
Relying on consumers to open support tickets promptly
optionNotes: Correct. The platform separates the concerns deliberately: API alerts watch the traffic's quality (latency, errors, policy breaches per API instance); infrastructure alerts watch the runtime (CPU, memory, restarts). An API can fail its SLA on perfectly healthy workers — both alert classes are needed.
Healthy CPU with failing responses (bad deploy, broken dependency) is common; resource metrics alone miss it.
Manual, periodic log reading is neither timely nor reliable paging.
Consumer complaints are the outcome alerting exists to prevent.
questions.explanation: Two alerting planes: API-quality metrics on the managed API instance, resource metrics on the deployment target. Wiring thresholds and notification channels on both gives operations coverage for logical failures and infrastructure failures alike.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. The application deploys successfully, but its API instance shows as 'Unregistered' in API Manager and no policies are enforced. Which cause best fits this configuration?
questions.exhibit:
<!-- global.xml -->
<api-gateway:autodiscovery apiId="${api.id}" flowRef="orders-main"/>

# deploy-time properties (prod)
api.id=17206543
anypoint.platform.client_id=6f2a...
anypoint.platform.client_secret=b91c...
options: The api.id value does not match the API instance ID for this environment
The flowRef names a private subflow instead of the app's main flow
Autodiscovery also needs the RAML to be re-published to Exchange first
The platform client_id and client_secret must be unique per worker
optionNotes: Correct — api.id must equal the target environment's instance ID; an id from another environment leaves the app unregistered.
A wrong flowRef fails the deployment rather than silently leaving the instance unregistered.
Re-publishing the spec isn't required for autodiscovery to bind a running app to its instance.
One Connected App credential pair is used per environment, not per worker.
questions.explanation: Autodiscovery binds a running app to its API instance via api.id plus platform credentials; the classic 'Unregistered' cause is an api.id that doesn't match the instance in that environment.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. The API must survive both a single worker failure and an availability-zone outage. What is the minimal change to this deployment?
questions.exhibit:
CloudHub deployment:
  workers: 1
  vCores: 1.0
  region: us-east-1
  persistent-queues: disabled
options: Increase to at least two workers so CloudHub spreads them across availability zones
Move the deployment to a different CloudHub region that offers more availability zones
Increase the single worker to 4 vCores to add processing headroom
Enable persistent queues so in-flight messages survive a restart
optionNotes: Correct — two or more workers are auto-distributed across AZs and load-balanced, covering both worker and AZ failure.
Every CloudHub region already provides multiple AZs; the gap is worker count, not region.
A bigger single worker is still one point of failure in one AZ.
Persistent queues protect in-flight messages, not the availability of the application.
questions.explanation: High availability on CloudHub comes from running multiple workers (auto-spread across AZs), not from vertical sizing or queue persistence.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. Where is each of these alerts configured?
questions.exhibit:
Alert requirements:
  A) page when API response-time p95 > 800 ms
  B) page when worker CPU > 85% for 5 min
  C) page when a policy is violated > 100x/min
options: A and C in API Manager (API-level); B in Runtime Manager / Anypoint Monitoring
All three configured in Runtime Manager, since it owns every runtime and API alert
All three configured in API Manager, since they all ultimately concern the API
A and B in API Manager; C in Runtime Manager as a security event
optionNotes: Correct — response-time and policy-violation alerts are API-Manager API alerts; worker CPU is Runtime Manager / Monitoring.
Runtime Manager doesn't own API response-time or policy-violation alerts.
Worker CPU is an infrastructure metric that API Manager doesn't alert on.
This reverses the split — CPU belongs in Runtime Manager and policy violations in API Manager.
questions.explanation: API-level SLA and policy alerts live in API Manager; worker/infrastructure alerts live in Runtime Manager and Anypoint Monitoring — different failure classes, different homes.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. A new 'Partner Orders' channel needs the same order-placement logic. Which addition follows API-led connectivity?
questions.exhibit:
Current design:
  Web xAPI     -> Orders pAPI -> Orders sAPI (SAP)
                              \-> Shipping sAPI (FedEx)
  Mobile xAPI  -> Orders pAPI
options: Add a Partner xAPI that calls the existing Orders pAPI, reusing its orchestration
Add a Partner xAPI that calls the Orders and Shipping sAPIs directly for speed
Duplicate the Orders pAPI orchestration inside a new Partner pAPI
Let the Partner channel call the Orders pAPI's SAP backend directly
optionNotes: Correct — a thin Experience API per channel reuses the one Process API; that reuse is exactly what the layers exist for.
Skipping the Process API scatters orchestration and consistency logic into the channel.
Duplicating the Process logic invites drift and double maintenance.
Channels must not reach past the layers into a System API's backend.
questions.explanation: New channels get a thin Experience API over the existing Process API; the orchestration lives once in the pAPI and is reused.

sections.title: Explaining Application Network Basics
objectives: Differentiate between technologies for implementing APIs
Describe the roles involved in a web API interaction
Assign APIs to the appropriate tier of an application network
Outline the capabilities and high-level components of Anypoint Platform
sections.notes:
API implementation technologies

An application network node is defined by its API contract, not its implementation. Implementations can be:

  • Mule applications — first-class on Anypoint Platform: APIkit scaffolding, autodiscovery, full policy support via the embedded gateway.
  • Non-Mule applications (Node.js, Java Spring, serverless functions…) — still part of the application network when fronted by an API proxy (deployed by API Manager) or Flex Gateway, which is where policies execute.

Architect's point: the platform governs any HTTP-speaking implementation; only where policies run changes (embedded in the Mule app vs a separate proxy/gateway process).

Roles in a web API interaction

RoleMeaning
API providerOrganization/team exposing the API (owns spec, implementation, instance)
API consumerThe application (client app) registered to call the API — identified by client_id
API clientThe software component actually making HTTP requests
API implementationThe running service behind the contract
API instanceA managed deployment of an API spec version in an environment (unit of policies/SLAs in API Manager)

Assigning APIs to tiers

Same three layers as everywhere in MuleSoft doctrine — the MCPA exam tests placement judgment:

  • System APIs unlock a single system of record; named after the system/domain object (Customers System API); insulate from system change; often high reuse.
  • Process APIs compose/orchestrate System (and other Process) APIs into business capabilities (Order Fulfillment PAPI); channel-agnostic; contain no channel formatting and no direct backend access.
  • Experience APIs adapt process/system data for one consumer channel (Mobile Orders xAPI); thin; owned ideally by the consuming team.

Exam tip: placement clues — "aggregates/orchestrates several systems" → Process; "one backend, canonical entity CRUD" → System; "shaped for the mobile app / partner portal" → Experience. An Experience API calling a backend directly, or a System API calling another System API's backend, are the classic anti-patterns.

Anypoint Platform capabilities

Same component map as MCD L1 s1 (Design Center, Exchange, API Manager, Runtime Manager, Access Management, Monitoring/Visualizer, runtimes). The architect view adds: Exchange as the discovery backbone of the application network, API Manager as the governance point, and the control-plane/runtime-plane split (metadata vs business data) as the basis of hosting decisions.

sections.title: Establishing Organizational and Platform Foundations
objectives: Advise on establishing a Center for Enablement (C4E)
Describe the structure and purpose of MuleSoft Catalyst
Select platform hosting options for given requirements
Compare identity management and client management options
Identify data residency of different kinds of data
sections.notes:
Center for Enablement (C4E) in practice

  • Cross-functional team (IT + lines of business) that enables instead of delivering: publishes reusable assets as products, sets and evangelizes standards, coaches project teams, and clears blockers.
  • Measured by consumption, not production: KPIs like % of assets reused per project, number of consumers per API, time-to-first-API for a new team, ratio of self-service vs C4E-delivered integrations.
  • Staffing evolves: seeded from the first delivery project's experts, then rotating champions from LOB teams; it is not a delivery bottleneck (that would be a traditional CoE).

MuleSoft Catalyst

Catalyst is MuleSoft's delivery methodology for achieving business outcomes, organized around three "plays": business outcomes (tie the application network to measurable KPIs), technology delivery (projects that build it), and organizational enablement (C4E, training, operating model). The Catalyst Knowledge Hub packages playbooks, templates, and best practices. Exam angle: given a struggling adoption scenario, identify which Catalyst pillar is missing (usually organizational enablement).

Platform hosting options and data residency

Data kindWhere it lives (MuleSoft-hosted control plane)
Management metadata (specs, configs, deployment info, monitoring metadata)The control plane region (US or EU) — choose the EU control plane for EU residency of metadata
Business data (payloads)Runtime plane only — CloudHub region of choice, RTF/customer-hosted = wherever you run it
Anypoint MQ messages / Object Store v2 contentThe runtime-plane region where provisioned (regional platform services)
Everything incl. control planeAnypoint Platform PCE (customer-hosted control plane) for the strictest residency/air-gap demands

Identity management vs client management

Identity managementClient management
WhoHumans — platform users (designers, ops)Applications — API consumers
ProtocoloSAML 2.0 / OpenID Connect SSO to the corporate IdPExternal OAuth 2.0 / OIDC client provider (Okta, Azure AD, Ping) issuing client credentials and tokens
EffectLogin federation, MFA at the IdPExchange access requests register clients in the external provider; gateways validate its tokens (OAuth/JWT policies)

Exam tip: "platform users must use corporate SSO" → identity management; "API consumers get credentials/tokens from our Okta" → client management. And: business data NEVER flows through the MuleSoft-hosted control plane — only metadata does.

sections.title: Designing and Sharing APIs
objectives: Identify dependencies between API specifications and implementations
Describe how assets are published, discovered, and reused via Exchange
Determine when a change requires which semantic version increment
Select strategies for mapping data models between bounded contexts
Recognize idempotent HTTP methods and HTTP-native optimistic concurrency
sections.notes:
API dependencies and consumption

  • The spec is the contract: consumers depend on the published RAML/OAS in Exchange, never on the implementation. Implementations also depend on the spec (APIkit validates against it), so the spec version is the pivot of change management.
  • Design-time dependencies: specs import API fragments (types, traits, examples) from Exchange; implementations depend on the spec version they scaffold from; client apps depend on the instance they hold contracts with.

Publishing and reusing assets in Exchange

  • Asset types: REST/SOAP/AsyncAPI specs, fragments, connectors, templates, examples, custom policies. Publication targets a business group; discoverability across groups is controlled by permissions.
  • Publishing a REST spec auto-generates the API console/portal, enables the mocking service, and (for REST) a REST Connect connector — the self-service consumption kit.

Semantic versioning decisions

ChangeVersion bump
Add optional field / new resource / new optional query paramMINOR (backwards compatible)
Fix docs/examples, clarify descriptionPATCH
Remove/rename a field, change a type, make optional required, change semanticsMAJOR — new API version, typically a new instance (e.g. /v2), parallel run + deprecation of the old

Only the MAJOR version surfaces to consumers (in the URL / instance); minor+patch evolve behind it.

Mapping between bounded contexts

Different business domains legitimately model the "same" entity differently (Sales' Customer ≠ Billing's Customer). Strategies the exam expects you to weigh:

  • Per-domain (bounded context) data models published as Exchange fragments; System/Process APIs translate at the boundary — the anti-corruption layer idea. Recommended default.
  • Enterprise (canonical) data model — one shared model for all: maximal consistency, but slow to agree and evolve; suits small orgs or genuinely shared core entities.
  • Direct pairwise mapping — fine for exactly two contexts, degrades combinatorially beyond that.

Idempotency and optimistic concurrency in HTTP

  • Idempotent methods: GET, PUT, DELETE (and HEAD/OPTIONS) — repeating them yields the same state, making retries safe. POST is not idempotent; safe retry of POST needs an idempotency key or dedup.
  • Optimistic concurrency, HTTP-native: the server returns an ETag (entity version); clients send If-Match: <etag> on updates. If the entity changed meanwhile, the server rejects with 412 Precondition Failed — no locks, lost updates prevented.

Exam tip: "two clients update the same record; the second must not silently overwrite the first" → ETag + If-Match (412). "Network timeout on a PUT — retry?" → yes, PUT is idempotent. "…on a POST?" → only with an idempotency mechanism.

sections.title: Designing APIs Using System, Process, and Experience Layers
objectives: Identify the APIs needed to implement a business process and assign them to layers
Recommend data model approaches per layer
Select when an Enterprise Data Model versus bounded-context models applies
Evaluate where Anypoint DataGraph is applicable
sections.notes:
Decomposing business processes into APIs

Given a scenario ("customer places an order; inventory is checked in SAP; the order is created in Salesforce; the mobile app shows status"), the expected decomposition:

  • System APIs: one per system of record touched — Inventory SAPI (SAP), Orders SAPI (Salesforce). CRUD-ish, canonical for their domain, reusable by any process.
  • Process API: Place Order PAPI orchestrating the SAPIs (check inventory → create order → trigger confirmation). Owns the business logic and its consistency strategy; no channel specifics.
  • Experience API: Mobile Orders xAPI shaping status responses for the app (field selection, pagination, mobile-friendly errors).

Heuristics: reuse pressure pushes logic DOWN (into Process/System); channel pressure pushes formatting UP (into Experience). A layer that only passes calls through unchanged is a candidate to remove — layers are a means, not an end.

Data models across the layers

LayerData model guidance
System APICanonical model of that system's bounded context — hides the backend's native format (IDoc, SOQL result…), exposes clean domain types
Process APIThe business process's own model, composed from the domain models it orchestrates
Experience APIThe consumer's model — exactly what the channel needs, nothing more

Enterprise Data Model vs bounded contexts

  • Enterprise Data Model (EDM): one org-wide definition per entity. Pros: no translation between domains. Cons: enormous coordination cost, slow change, lowest-common-denominator types. Realistic mainly for small orgs or a handful of truly universal entities.
  • Bounded-context models (recommended default): each business domain owns its model, published to Exchange; translations happen explicitly in APIs at context boundaries. Change stays local; teams move independently.
  • Hybrid is common: bounded contexts + a small shared kernel (e.g. Money, Address types) as Exchange fragments.

Anypoint DataGraph

  • DataGraph exposes one GraphQL endpoint over multiple existing REST APIs in the application network: consumers query exactly the fields they need across related entities in a single request, instead of calling several APIs and stitching results.
  • Applicable when: consumers (typically Experience-layer needs) aggregate slices of several REST APIs, the source APIs are already managed and stable, and the queries are read-oriented.
  • Not applicable for: writes/orchestration with business logic (that's a Process API), event-driven flows, or replacing API-led layers wholesale.

Exam tip: DataGraph = read aggregation without building a new API. If the scenario involves updates, transactions, or business rules, the answer is a Process API, not DataGraph.

sections.title: Governing Web APIs on Anypoint Platform
objectives: Manage API instances across environments
Select API policies to meet non-functional requirements
Specify how policies are enforced (embedded gateway, proxy, Flex Gateway)
Explain how consumers pass client ID/secret and how access is granted
Identify scenarios requiring custom policies
sections.notes:
API instances across environments

  • One spec version → an API instance per environment (dev/test/prod) in API Manager, each with its own api.id, policies, SLA tiers, contracts, and alerts. Implementations pair via autodiscovery with the environment-specific id.
  • Promotion: API Manager can promote an instance from a lower environment, carrying policies/tiers/alerts — keeping governance consistent without manual re-setup.

Selecting policies for NFRs

NFRPolicy
Identify/limit consumers per contractClient ID enforcement + SLA-based rate limiting
Protect backend from burstsSpike control (smooths) / rate limiting (caps)
AuthN/AuthZ of callersOAuth 2.0 enforcement, JWT validation, Basic auth
Network restrictionIP allowlist/blocklist
Browser cross-originCORS
Reduce backend readsHTTP caching policy
Payload hygieneHeader injection/removal, message size limits (custom/edge)

Policies can be resource-level (specific methods/paths) and are ordered; automated policies apply org/environment-wide to every API instance (e.g. mandatory TLS or auth baseline) — governance at scale.

Policy enforcement options

OptionWhere policies runUse when
Embedded gateway (autodiscovery)Inside the Mule app itselfMule implementations — no extra hop or process
API proxyA separate Mule proxy app generated by API ManagerNon-Mule backends, or implementations you can't modify
Flex GatewayLightweight standalone gateway (Envoy-based)High-performance edge for any backend, Kubernetes/ingress patterns

Passing client ID and secret

  • Client ID enforcement is configurable to read credentials from custom headers (commonly client_id/client_secret), query parameters (discouraged — IDs leak into logs/URLs), or HTTP Basic auth. The API spec should document the chosen mechanism (a shared RAML trait keeps it consistent).
  • Failure modes: missing/invalid credentials → 401; valid credentials but over the tier's limit → 429; no contract with the API → 401/403 per policy.

SLA tiers, contracts, and access management

  1. Provider defines SLA tiers (limits + auto/manual approval) on the instance.
  2. Consumer discovers the API in Exchange, requests access with a client application, selecting a tier.
  3. Approval creates a contract; the client app holds client_id/secret (issued by Anypoint or the external client provider).
  4. Gateways enforce per-contract limits; analytics attribute traffic per consumer.

Custom policies

When included policies don't cover the requirement — e.g. a proprietary auth scheme, request signing validation, custom audit headers, payload field enforcement — build a custom policy, publish it to Exchange, and apply it like any other. Prefer included policies first; custom policies carry maintenance/upgrade cost.

Exam tip: the 17% section. Drill: policy ↔ NFR matching; the three enforcement options and when each applies; the Exchange access-request → contract → credentials flow; 401 vs 429 semantics; automated policies for org-wide mandates.

sections.title: Architecting and Deploying API Implementations
objectives: Explain how autodiscovery links an implementation to its API instance
Compare hosting options for API implementations
Identify requirements that call for an Anypoint VPC
Distinguish unit testing from integration testing responsibilities
Describe options for automated building, testing, and deployment
sections.notes:
Autodiscovery: linking implementation to instance

  • The Mule app declares api-gateway:autodiscovery with the instance's api.id (property per environment) and a reference to the main flow; the runtime authenticates to the platform (client id/secret of the environment) and registers.
  • Result: API Manager shows the instance Active; the embedded gateway pulls and enforces policies, reports analytics, and tracks contracts — no proxy hop.
  • Common failure causes: wrong api.id for the environment, missing platform client credentials on the runtime, spec/implementation mismatch.

Choosing runtime plane hosting

RequirementPoints to
Minimum ops burden, fast startCloudHub (1.0/2.0)
Containers on customer cloud/K8s, platform-managedRuntime Fabric
Full control on own servers; domains/clustersCustomer-hosted runtimes
Strict data residency for payloadsRuntime plane in-region (any option) — control plane choice is separate

When an Anypoint VPC is required

Indicators that plain multi-tenant CloudHub networking is NOT enough:

  • APIs must be private (reachable only from corporate networks) → VPC + internal endpoints (8091/8092).
  • Connectivity to on-prem systems over VPN / DirectConnect / peering → VPC terminates it.
  • Custom domains / custom TLS / mutual TLS → Dedicated Load Balancer, which requires a VPC.
  • Firewall control over which ports/paths reach workers → VPC firewall rules.

Testing: unit vs integration

  • Unit (MUnit): flow logic with mocked dependencies; runs in every build; coverage gates. Owned by the implementing team.
  • Integration: deployed app against real dependencies (auth, TLS, contracts, queues) in a test environment; API tests against the instance (policies included). Validates the interactions unit tests mock away.
  • The API spec enables consumer-side testing too: mocking service for early consumers, contract tests that fail when implementation drifts from spec.

Automated build, test, and deployment

  • Maven-centric: mule-maven-plugin (package + deploy to CH/CH2/RTF/hybrid), munit-maven-plugin (tests + coverage), Exchange as artifact repository — the canonical CI/CD spine, driven by any CI server.
  • Platform APIs / Anypoint CLI: automate API Manager (create instances, apply policies), Exchange publication, Runtime Manager operations.
  • Credentials: Connected Apps; per-environment configuration injected at deploy time; build once, promote the artifact.

Exam tip: autodiscovery questions hinge on: environment-specific api.id + platform credentials on the runtime + policies enforced INSIDE the app. VPC questions hinge on: private access, VPN, DLB/custom TLS — any of those words means VPC.

sections.title: Deploying API Implementations to CloudHub
objectives: Select worker sizes and counts for requirements
Predict the reliability characteristics of CloudHub deployments
Determine appropriate uses of Object Store v2
Differentiate the shared and dedicated load balancers
Compare CloudHub networking options
Design deployments without single points of failure
sections.notes:
Workers, sizing, and scaling

  • Workers are dedicated per-app VMs, sized in vCores (0.1 / 0.2 / 1 / 2 / 4 / 8 / 16). Vertical scaling = bigger worker (more memory/CPU per instance — helps large payloads, heavy transforms); horizontal scaling = more workers (throughput + availability; requires the app to be effectively stateless or use shared state services).
  • Multi-worker apps are load-balanced automatically and workers are distributed across availability zones; CloudHub restarts unresponsive workers and offers zero-downtime redeploys (new workers up before old ones stop).

Reliability on CloudHub

MechanismWhat it protects
≥2 workersWorker/AZ failure — the surviving worker keeps serving (HA)
Persistent queues (CH 1.0 feature)VM-queued messages survive worker restarts and are shared across the app's workers
Anypoint MQDurable inter-app messaging with DLQs
Object Store v2State shared across workers, surviving restarts (30-day max TTL, regional)
Multi-region deployment + DNSRegion loss (DR) — nothing is automatic cross-region

Object Store v2 on CloudHub

Right uses: watermarks, dedup/idempotency keys, cached lookups, tokens, small shared state across workers. Wrong uses: business database, message queue, large blobs (10 MB value cap), anything needing >30-day retention. It is rate-limited and REGIONAL (DR needs a plan).

Shared vs dedicated load balancers

Shared LB (default)Dedicated LB
Domain / TLS*.<region>.cloudhub.io with MuleSoft's certificateCustom domains, YOUR certificates, TLS termination control
Mutual TLS from clientsNoYes (client cert validation)
Mapping rules / URL rewritingNoYes
Requires VPCNoYes (deployed into your VPC; scalable, multiple instances for HA)

CloudHub networking choices

Recap of the decision ladder (details in the Integration Architect notes): public app → default; partner allowlisting → static IPs; private/internal APIs → VPC + internal endpoints (8091/8092); on-prem connectivity → VPC + VPN/DirectConnect; custom domain/mTLS → VPC + DLB. CloudHub 2.0 equivalents: private spaces + ingress.

Eliminating single points of failure

  • One worker is a SPOF — always ≥2 for production HA.
  • One DLB instance can be scaled to multiple; the shared LB is inherently redundant.
  • State: keep it out of worker memory (OSv2 / MQ / DB), or a worker loss loses it.
  • Dependencies count too: a single backend, a single VPN tunnel, or a single region are SPOFs the Mule design must acknowledge (fallbacks, retries, DR plan).

Exam tip: "custom TLS certificate" or "partner requires mutual TLS" → DLB (thus VPC). "Survive a worker failure with no message loss" → 2+ workers AND persistent queues/MQ (workers alone don't persist in-flight VM messages). Worker sizing questions: memory-heavy → vertical; TPS-heavy → horizontal.

sections.title: Meeting API Quality Goals
objectives: Design caching with the Cache scope and the HTTP Caching policy
Choose between Object Store state and response caching
Select resilience strategies for API clients
Describe how horizontal scaling supports quality goals
sections.notes:
Caching strategies: Cache scope vs HTTP caching policy

Cache scope (in the Mule flow)HTTP Caching policy (at the gateway)
WhereInside the implementation, around expensive processorsIn front of the API — requests may never reach the implementation
GranularityAny key expression, any sub-result (e.g. one backend call of several)Whole HTTP responses, keyed by request; honors/controls HTTP caching semantics
Configured byDevelopers (flow design)API owners (API Manager, no redeploy)

Both need the same architect decisions: what is safely cacheable (idempotent reads, tolerable staleness), key design, TTL, and invalidation. On multi-worker apps the Cache scope needs an OSv2-backed caching strategy for consistency.

Object Store vs caching

The Object Store is a state store; a cache is an optimization whose content can vanish anytime without breaking correctness. Watermarks and dedup IDs are STATE (losing them causes reprocessing/duplicates) — they belong in an Object Store with deliberate TTLs, not in a cache. A cached price list is a CACHE — losing it only costs a recomputation.

Client resilience patterns

  • Timeouts — always bounded; a client without response timeouts inherits every downstream hang.
  • Retries with backoff — for transient failures, ONLY on idempotent operations (or with idempotency keys); Until Successful / reconnection strategies in Mule.
  • Circuit breaker — stop hammering a failing dependency; fail fast and recover probing later (Anypoint MQ subscribers have one built in; otherwise implemented via Object Store state or custom policy).
  • Fallbacks — cached/default responses or a degraded path when the dependency is down (First Successful router, cached last-known-good).
  • Bulkheading/limits — maxConcurrency bounds how much load one dependency's slowness can consume.

Horizontal scaling for quality goals

  • More workers/replicas raise throughput roughly linearly for stateless APIs and improve availability (surviving instance keeps serving) — but do NOT reduce single-request latency (that needs caching/parallelism/fewer hops).
  • Prerequisites: stateless design (shared state in OSv2/DB/MQ), safe concurrent consumption (idempotency), and backends able to absorb the scaled traffic (or throttling toward them).

Exam tip: classic discriminators — "reduce load on the backend for repeated identical GETs, configurable without redeploying" → HTTP Caching policy; "avoid recomputing one expensive lookup inside the flow" → Cache scope; "remember the last processed record" → Object Store (state, not cache); "faster single request" → NOT more workers.

sections.title: Monitoring and Analyzing Application Networks
objectives: Identify the sources of monitoring data on Anypoint Platform
Describe API invocation metrics and what they reveal
Specify alerts for APIs at every layer of the network
Define alerts for Mule applications
sections.notes:
Sources of monitoring data

SourceData
API Manager analytics (gateways)Per-API-instance invocation data: requests, response codes, latency, policy violations, per-consumer (client id) attribution
Anypoint MonitoringApp + API dashboards (throughput, response time, errors, JVM/CPU), custom dashboards, log management (Titanium), threshold alerts
Runtime ManagerApp/worker status, deployment events, basic metrics, operational alerts
API Functional Monitoring (BAT)Scheduled black-box calls asserting real behavior from public/private locations
Anypoint VisualizerThe live application network graph built from actual traffic — dependencies, layers, troubleshooting blast radius
Application logsBusiness/technical events with correlation IDs (centralized via log management or external systems)

API invocation metrics and analytics

  • Core metrics per instance: request volume (trend = adoption; also the reuse KPI for the C4E), response times (p95/p99 vs SLA), error ratios (4xx = client/contract problems, 5xx = provider problems), policy violations (429s = tiers too tight or abuse), and per-consumer breakdowns (who drives load; who a breaking change affects).
  • Analytics inform lifecycle decisions: which APIs to invest in, which versions can retire (traffic on deprecated instances), and chargeback/showback per consumer.

Alerts across the layers

Alert at EVERY layer of the network, aligned to each API's SLA — an Experience API breach may originate two layers down:

  • Per API instance (API Manager): response time thresholds, error-code ratios, request-count anomalies, policy violations.
  • Experience layer: tightest latency/error alerts (closest to user experience) + functional monitors simulating consumers.
  • Process/System layers: same metric alerts with layer-appropriate thresholds; System API alerts often reveal backend degradation first.

Alerts for Mule applications

  • Runtime Manager: application down / not responding, worker not responding, deployment failed, secure-data gateway events, custom application notifications.
  • Anypoint Monitoring: threshold alerts on any collected metric (CPU, memory, error rate, response time) with notification channels.
  • Design guidance: alert on symptoms tied to SLAs (latency, error ratio, availability), keep thresholds per environment, and route to owners (API owner vs platform ops) — an unowned alert is noise.

Exam tip: map the question to the source — "which consumers call this API and how often?" → API Manager analytics; "is the app's JVM memory climbing?" → Anypoint Monitoring; "did the deployment fail last night?" → Runtime Manager; "is the API returning correct data right now?" → functional monitoring (BAT); "what depends on this System API?" → Visualizer.

cards.front: Non-Mule service must be governed by API Manager — options?
cards.back: Front it with an API proxy (Mule proxy app) or FLEX GATEWAY (Envoy-based); policies execute at the gateway. Mule apps use the embedded gateway via autodiscovery.

cards.front: API instance — definition?
cards.back: A managed deployment of one API spec VERSION in one ENVIRONMENT: the unit carrying api.id, policies, SLA tiers, contracts, alerts, analytics.

cards.front: Layer placement clues in scenarios?
cards.back: Orchestrates/aggregates systems → Process. One backend, canonical CRUD → System. Channel-shaped output → Experience. Experience→backend directly = anti-pattern.

cards.front: C4E health KPIs?
cards.back: Consumption-based: % of projects reusing assets, consumers per API, time-to-first-API for new teams — NOT how much the C4E builds itself.

cards.front: MuleSoft Catalyst's three plays?
cards.back: Business outcomes, technology delivery, organizational enablement — with playbooks in the Catalyst Knowledge Hub.

cards.front: Data residency: what does the control plane see vs the runtime plane?
cards.back: Control plane: management METADATA only (choose US/EU control plane for its residency). Business payloads: runtime plane only. MQ/OSv2 data: regional runtime services.

cards.front: ETag + If-Match flow?
cards.back: Server returns ETag (entity version) → client sends If-Match on update → stale version rejected with 412 Precondition Failed. HTTP-native optimistic concurrency.

cards.front: What does Exchange auto-generate when a REST spec is published?
cards.back: API console/portal documentation, mocking service endpoint, and a REST Connect connector.

cards.front: Which spec changes force a MAJOR version?
cards.back: Remove/rename fields, type changes, optional→required, semantic changes — breaking. New optional fields/resources = MINOR; doc fixes = PATCH.

cards.front: When is Anypoint DataGraph the right answer? When not?
cards.back: Right: READ-ONLY aggregation across existing managed REST APIs with client-selected fields (one GraphQL query). Wrong: writes, business rules, orchestration → Process API.

cards.front: Where do reuse pressure and channel pressure push logic?
cards.back: Reuse pushes logic DOWN (Process/System APIs, once). Channel pressure pushes formatting UP (thin Experience APIs per channel).

cards.front: A pure pass-through Process API — verdict?
cards.back: Question it: layers must earn their hop (latency + ops cost). Collapsing is a justified exception — API-led is guidance, not liturgy.

cards.front: Spike control vs rate limiting?
cards.back: Rate limiting REJECTS over-quota requests (429). Spike control SMOOTHS bursts (queues/delays) to protect the backend without hard rejection.

cards.front: Org-wide mandatory policies on every current AND future API?
cards.back: AUTOMATED POLICIES applied at organization/environment level — governance at scale, no reliance on each team remembering.

cards.front: Why not pass client_id/secret as query parameters?
cards.back: URLs leak into caches, proxies, access logs, browser history. Use custom HEADERS or Basic auth (configurable on the policy).

cards.front: Consumer access flow to a governed API?
cards.back: Exchange: find API → request access with a CLIENT APPLICATION → choose SLA tier → contract approved → use issued client_id/secret. Gateway enforces per-contract limits.

cards.front: Custom policy — when justified?
cards.back: Requirement not covered by included policies (proprietary auth/signing, bespoke audit headers). Build, publish to Exchange, apply like any policy. Prefer included first.

cards.front: API instance shows 'Unregistered' — three usual causes?
cards.back: Wrong api.id for the environment; missing/wrong platform client credentials on the runtime; autodiscovery flowRef not pointing at the main flow.

cards.front: Requirements that force an Anypoint VPC?
cards.back: Private (corp-only) APIs, VPN/DirectConnect to on-prem, Dedicated Load Balancer (custom domain/mTLS), firewall control of worker access.

cards.front: Vertical vs horizontal worker scaling — when each?
cards.back: Vertical (bigger vCores): memory/CPU-heavy per-request work (large payloads). Horizontal (more workers): throughput + high availability; needs stateless design.

cards.front: CloudHub HA features you get by deploying ≥2 workers?
cards.back: Automatic load balancing, workers spread across availability zones, platform restarts unresponsive workers, zero-downtime redeploys.

cards.front: Which state survives a worker crash?
cards.back: Object Store v2 entries and persistent-queue messages. In-memory VM queues, in-flight events, worker memory: GONE. Persist-before-process for zero loss.

cards.front: Shared vs Dedicated LB quick contrast?
cards.back: SLB: *.cloudhub.io, MuleSoft cert, no client-cert validation, no mapping rules, free. DLB: custom domains + your certs + mTLS + mapping rules; needs VPC; scale instances for HA.

cards.front: HTTP Caching policy vs Cache scope?
cards.back: Policy: whole responses cached AT THE GATEWAY, configured in API Manager, no code change. Cache scope: any sub-result INSIDE the flow, developer-designed keys/TTL.

cards.front: State vs cache — the losability test?
cards.back: Cache: losable without correctness impact (memoized lookups, tokens). State: losing it breaks correctness (watermarks, dedup keys) → Object Store with deliberate TTL.

cards.front: Client resilience toolkit?
cards.back: Bounded timeouts everywhere; retries with backoff on IDEMPOTENT calls; circuit breaker (fail fast while unhealthy); fallback to last-known-good; maxConcurrency bulkheads.

cards.front: Does horizontal scaling reduce single-request latency?
cards.back: No — it raises THROUGHPUT and availability. Latency needs caching, parallel calls, or fewer hops.

cards.front: Monitoring source map: consumer breakdown / JVM trends / call graph / correctness now?
cards.back: API Manager analytics / Anypoint Monitoring / Visualizer / API Functional Monitoring (BAT).

cards.front: Rising 429s in analytics — what does it mean?
cards.back: Consumers exceeding SLA tiers: either legitimate growth (resize tiers) or abuse (engage/restrict the client) — identify WHO via per-consumer analytics. Not a backend error.

cards.front: Why alert at every API layer, not just Experience?
cards.back: Layer-specific alerts LOCALIZE faults — a System API breach often precedes and explains the Experience-layer breach (backend degradation shows lowest first).

cards.front: Runtime Manager vs API Manager alerts?
cards.back: RM: app/worker down, deployment failed (operational lifecycle). APIM: policy violations, response codes, latency thresholds per API instance.

cards.front: Business-level monitoring (orders/hour) — mechanism?
cards.back: Custom Business Events or structured log metrics feeding custom dashboards/alerts — technical health ≠ business health.
